top_openings.name: Sicilian Defence
top_openings.reason: Creates unbalanced games with lots of winning chances.
top_openings.plan: Fight for the centre from the side and look for counterplay instead of copying White.
top_openings.mistakeToAvoid: Do not ignore development while chasing tactics.
top_openings.publicModeNote: This appears to be a high-level or public account. OpeningFit is analysing recent online results only, not judging the player’s actual opening knowledge.

top_openings.name: Caro-Kann Defence
top_openings.reason: Good if you want a solid structure with fewer early traps.
top_openings.plan: Build a strong centre, develop safely and counterattack later.
top_openings.mistakeToAvoid: Do not play too passively after completing development.
top_openings.difficulty: Easy to learn, harder to master
top_openings.publicModeNote: This appears to be a high-level or public account. OpeningFit is analysing recent online results only, not judging the player’s actual opening knowledge.

top_openings.name: Scandinavian Defence
top_openings.reason: Good for players who want direct positions and simple development plans.
top_openings.plan: Challenge the centre early, develop quickly and avoid wasting queen moves.
top_openings.mistakeToAvoid: Do not move the queen too many times in the opening.
top_openings.publicModeNote: This appears to be a high-level or public account. OpeningFit is analysing recent online results only, not judging the player’s actual opening knowledge.

top_openings.name: French Defence
top_openings.reason: Fits solid players who like structure and counterattacking pawn breaks.
top_openings.plan: Challenge White's centre with ...c5 and develop with a clear pawn structure.
top_openings.mistakeToAvoid: Do not trap your light-squared bishop forever.
top_openings.publicModeNote: This appears to be a high-level or public account. OpeningFit is analysing recent online results only, not judging the player’s actual opening knowledge.

top_openings.name: Queen Pawn Game
top_openings.reason: Good for players who like stable centres and long-term pressure.
top_openings.plan: Control the centre, develop calmly and prepare pressure on the queenside or centre.
top_openings.mistakeToAvoid: Do not grab pawns if it slows your development too much.
top_openings.publicModeNote: This appears to be a high-level or public account. OpeningFit is analysing recent online results only, not judging the player’s actual opening knowledge.

top_openings.name: Vienna Game
top_openings.reason: Fits players who like quick development, attacking chances and natural piece play.
top_openings.plan: Develop with Nc3, Bc4 and aim for pressure on the centre or f7.
top_openings.mistakeToAvoid: Do not attack too early before your king is safe.
top_openings.publicModeNote: This appears to be a high-level or public account. OpeningFit is analysing recent online results only, not judging the player’s actual opening knowledge.

recommendations: This appears to be a high-level or public account. OpeningFit is analysing recent online results only, not judging the player’s actual opening knowledge.
As White, the most common recent sample is Vienna Game.
As Black, the most common recent sample is Modern Defence.
Queen Pawn Game as White is too small or noisy a sample for a hard verdict.
You seem to score best when you play actively rather than passively.

style_profile.summary: You lean toward active positions and practical initiative. Your games suggest you are comfortable in sharper positions. You seem comfortable transposing between different setups.

training_plan: As White, Vienna Game is a recent recurring sample to review by move order and opponent pool.
As Black, review the recent Modern Defence sample as an online-results trend, not a knowledge verdict.
Queen Pawn Game as White is too small a sample for a hard verdict.
Study 2 tactical motifs from your main openings and look for initiative before material.
After each game, review where tactics first appeared rather than only the final blunder.
Choose one main line and one backup line so flexibility does not become inconsistency.
